words.shortDefinition: The smallest deployable units of computing that you can create and manage in Kubernetes.
words.simpleDefinition: One or more containers that run together on the same machine, share networking and storage. The atomic unit in Kubernetes.
words.longDefinition: A group of one or more containers, with shared storage and network resources, and a specification for how to run the containers. Containers within a Pod are always co-located and co-scheduled, and run in a shared context.
words.connector: Think of pods like an apartment — containers are roommates sharing the same network (Wi-Fi) and storage (fridge), always on the same floor.
words.techContext: You rarely create individual Pods directly; instead, you create them using workload resources like Deployments or jobs.
words.exampleSentence: We noticed that one of our pods was stuck in the 'Pending' state due to insufficient CPU resources on the cluster.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a pod of whales or a pea pod (a group of related things).

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.14.2

words.shortDefinition: A worker machine in Kubernetes, which may be either a virtual or a physical machine.
words.simpleDefinition: The actual servers (VMs or physical boxes) that run your pods — the worker machines in the cluster.
words.longDefinition: Each node contains the services necessary to run Pods, including the container runtime, kubelet, and kube-proxy. The Control Plane manages the nodes and the Pods across them.
words.connector: Think of nodes like warehouse workers — the control plane (manager) assigns tasks (pods) to them, and they do the actual work.
words.techContext: The capacity of your cluster is determined by the total resources (CPU/RAM) available across all your nodes.
words.exampleSentence: We added two more nodes to the cluster to handle the increased traffic during the Black Friday sale.

words.shortDefinition: An agent that runs on each node in the cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: The agent running on each node that talks to the control plane, starts/stops containers, and reports back on health.
words.longDefinition: It makes sure that containers are running in a Pod. The kubelet takes a set of PodSpecs that are provided through various mechanisms and ensures that the containers described in those PodSpecs are running and healthy.
words.connector: Think of kubelet like a foreman on a construction site — takes orders from the boss (control plane), makes sure workers (containers) are doing their jobs.
words.techContext: If a node shows as 'NotReady', the first thing we check is whether the kubelet process is still running on that machine.
words.exampleSentence: The kubelet reported a failure because it couldn't pull the container image from the private registry.

words.shortDefinition: An API object that manages external access to the services in a cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: The front door for HTTP/HTTPS traffic — routes requests from the internet to the right service inside your cluster.
words.longDefinition: Typically provides HTTP/HTTPS routing. Ingress may provide load balancing, SSL termination, and name-based virtual hosting. It acts as the 'front door' for traffic entering the Kubernetes cluster.
words.connector: Think of Ingress like a hotel receptionist — all guests (traffic) come through the lobby, and the receptionist routes them to the right room (service).
words.techContext: We configured an Ingress rule to route all traffic for 'api.example.com' to our backend service.
words.exampleSentence: By using an Ingress controller like Nginx, we can manage all our SSL certificates in one place instead of for each individual service.

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: myapp.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: my-service
            port: { number: 80 }

words.shortDefinition: A container that runs alongside the main application container in a Pod.
words.simpleDefinition: A helper container that runs next to your main container in the same pod — handles logging, monitoring, or proxying without cluttering your app code.
words.longDefinition: A design pattern where a secondary container enhances or extends the functionality of the primary container (e.g., logging agents, service mesh proxies like Istio, or config watchers).
words.connector: Think of a sidecar like a personal assistant — sits next to you (in the same pod), handles tedious stuff (logs, metrics) so you can focus on your main job.
words.techContext: We added a sidecar container to our Pod to automatically ship all application logs to Elasticsearch.
words.exampleSentence: The sidecar pattern allows us to separate cross-cutting concerns like security and telemetry from our main business logic.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a sidecar attached to a motorcycle.

words.shortDefinition: A resource for managing declarative updates to Pods and ReplicaSets.
words.simpleDefinition: Declare how many copies of your app you want running, what version, and Kubernetes makes it happen — handles updates and rollbacks automatically.
words.longDefinition: You describe a desired state in a Deployment, and the Deployment Controller changes the actual state to the desired state at a controlled rate. It handles rolling updates and rollbacks automatically.
words.connector: Think of Deployment like a thermostat — you set the desired state (72°F / 3 replicas), and it continuously works to maintain that state.
words.techContext: Using a Deployment is the standard way to run stateless web applications in Kubernetes.
words.exampleSentence: I triggered a new deployment to roll out the updated version of our frontend with zero downtime.

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: my-app:v2

words.shortDefinition: Ensures that a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time.
words.simpleDefinition: Keeps exactly N copies of a pod running — if one dies, it spins up a replacement immediately.
words.longDefinition: A ReplicaSet's purpose is to maintain a stable set of replica Pods running at any given time. As such, it is often used to guarantee the availability of a specified number of identical Pods.
words.connector: Think of ReplicaSet like a lifeguard with multiple buoys — keeps exactly 5 buoys floating, if one sinks, tosses out a replacement.
words.techContext: While you can use ReplicaSets directly, it's recommended to use Deployments instead, as they provide high-level management for ReplicaSets.
words.exampleSentence: The ReplicaSet automatically spun up a new pod when one of the existing ones crashed due to an out-of-memory error.

words.shortDefinition: An abstract way to expose an application running on a set of Pods as a network service.
words.simpleDefinition: A stable DNS name and IP for a group of pods — pods come and go, but the Service address stays constant.
words.longDefinition: Since Pods are ephemeral, their IP addresses change. A Service provides a single, stable IP address and DNS name to reach a group of Pods, acting as a basic load balancer within the cluster.
words.connector: Think of Service like a company phone number — employees (pods) come and go, but customers always call the same number and get routed to whoever's available.
words.techContext: We created a Service of type 'ClusterIP' so that our frontend pod can always find the backend pod at 'backend-service:8080'.
words.exampleSentence: The frontend was unable to connect to the backend because the Service selector didn't match the labels on the backend pods.
words.etymology: from Latin servitium ('condition of a slave/servant').

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-app
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 3000

words.shortDefinition: An API object used to store non-confidential data in key-value pairs.
words.simpleDefinition: Store config like database URLs or feature flags separately from your code — inject it into pods as env vars or files.
words.longDefinition: Pods can consume ConfigMaps as environment variables, command-line arguments, or as configuration files in a volume. This allows you to decouple environment-specific configuration from your container images.
words.connector: Think of ConfigMaps like .env files — config lives outside the code, same image works in dev/staging/prod with different config.
words.techContext: We use a ConfigMap to store the database URL and API endpoints for each of our environments (dev, staging, prod).
words.exampleSentence: I updated the ConfigMap to change the application's log level without needing to rebuild the Docker image.

words.shortDefinition: An object that contains a small amount of sensitive data such as a password or a token.
words.simpleDefinition: Like a ConfigMap but for sensitive data — passwords, API keys, certs. Base64-encoded, kept out of your container images.
words.longDefinition: Similar to a ConfigMap but specifically intended to hold sensitive information. Secrets are stored base64-encoded by default and can be mounted into pods to keep credentials out of your application code and image.
words.connector: Think of Secrets like a password manager — stores credentials separately from the app, injects them at runtime, never hardcoded.
words.techContext: Never put API keys in a ConfigMap; always use a Kubernetes Secret to manage sensitive tokens.
words.exampleSentence: The app couldn't connect to the database because the DB_PASSWORD secret hadn't been created in the new namespace yet.

words.shortDefinition: A virtual cluster within a Kubernetes cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: Isolates resources into virtual clusters — different teams, environments, or projects share the same physical cluster but stay separated.
words.longDefinition: Used to isolate groups of resources. Namespaces are intended for use in environments with many users spread across multiple teams or projects. They provide a scope for names and can have resource quotas.
words.connector: Think of namespaces like departments in a company — same building (cluster), but marketing and engineering have separate spaces and budgets.
words.techContext: We created separate 'staging' and 'production' namespaces to ensure that a mistake in staging wouldn't affect our live users.
words.exampleSentence: You need to switch your context to the 'monitoring' namespace to see the Prometheus pods.

words.shortDefinition: A piece of storage in the cluster that has been provisioned by an administrator.
words.simpleDefinition: A chunk of storage (like an EBS disk) that outlives pods — data persists even when the pod dies and restarts.
words.longDefinition: Often abbreviated as PV. It is a resource in the cluster just as a node is a cluster resource. PVs have a lifecycle independent of any individual Pod that uses the PV.
words.connector: Think of PersistentVolumes like an external hard drive — pods are computers that come and go, but the drive keeps your files safe.
words.techContext: We use PersistentVolumes backed by AWS EBS to ensure our database data is safe even if the database pod is moved to a different node.
words.exampleSentence: The PersistentVolume remained available even after the pod was deleted, allowing the new pod to claim the same data.

words.shortDefinition: A request for storage by a user.
words.simpleDefinition: A request for storage — 'I need 10GB' — and Kubernetes finds or creates a PersistentVolume to match.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as PVC. It is similar to a Pod. Pods consume node resources and PVCs consume PV resources. Claims can request specific size and access modes (e.g., ReadWriteOnce).
words.connector: Think of PVC like ordering storage on demand — you request 10GB, Kubernetes provisions it from the pool of available disks.
words.techContext: The developer creates a PVC, and Kubernetes automatically finds or creates a matching PV to satisfy the request.
words.exampleSentence: I had to increase the storage request in the PersistentVolumeClaim because our database was running out of disk space.

words.shortDefinition: Ensures that all (or some) Nodes run a copy of a Pod.
words.simpleDefinition: Runs one copy of a pod on every node automatically — great for log collectors, monitoring agents, or security scanners.
words.longDefinition: As nodes are added to the cluster, Pods are added to them. As nodes are removed from the cluster, those Pods are garbage collected. Deleting a DaemonSet will clean up the Pods it created.
words.connector: Think of DaemonSet like sprinklers on every floor of a building — new floor added? Sprinkler automatically installed.
words.techContext: Commonly used for cluster-wide background tasks like log collection (Fluentd) or node monitoring (Prometheus Node Exporter).
words.exampleSentence: We deployed the security scanner as a DaemonSet to ensure every single node in our cluster is being monitored for vulnerabilities.

words.shortDefinition: Manages the deployment and scaling of a set of Pods with unique, persistent identities.
words.simpleDefinition: Like a Deployment but pods have stable names and storage — pod 'db-0' is always 'db-0', even after restart. For databases.
words.longDefinition: Unlike a Deployment, a StatefulSet maintains a sticky identity for each of its Pods. These pods are created from the same spec, but are not interchangeable; each has a persistent identifier that it maintains across any rescheduling.
words.connector: Think of StatefulSet like reserved parking spots — each car (pod) has its assigned spot number that never changes, vs Deployment where any open spot works.
words.techContext: Essential for running databases or distributed systems like Kafka or MongoDB that require stable network IDs and persistent storage.
words.exampleSentence: We switched our Redis cluster to a StatefulSet so each node would maintain its DNS name (e.g., redis-0, redis-1) even after a restart.

words.shortDefinition: The package manager for Kubernetes.
words.simpleDefinition: Package manager for Kubernetes — bundle dozens of YAML files into a reusable chart, install complex apps with one command.
words.longDefinition: Helm helps you manage Kubernetes applications — Helm Charts help you define, install, and upgrade even the most complex Kubernetes application.
words.connector: Think of Helm like apt or homebrew — install a whole app stack with 'helm install nginx' instead of manually applying 20 YAML files.
words.techContext: Instead of writing dozens of YAML files, we used a Helm chart to install the entire monitoring stack with one command.
words.exampleSentence: I used Helm to upgrade our ingress controller to the latest version while keeping our custom configuration values.
words.etymology: Metaphor from nautical 'helm' (a tiller or wheel for steering a ship).

words.shortDefinition: The Kubernetes command-line tool.
words.simpleDefinition: The CLI for Kubernetes — 'kubectl get pods', 'kubectl apply -f', 'kubectl logs'. Your daily driver for cluster management.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to run commands against Kubernetes clusters. You can use kubectl to deploy applications, inspect and manage cluster resources, and view logs.
words.connector: Think of kubectl like git — the essential command-line tool you use for every operation, from checking status to deploying changes.
words.techContext: The daily tool for any K8s engineer. Common commands include 'get pods', 'describe service', and 'apply -f'.
words.exampleSentence: I used 'kubectl logs' to investigate why the payment processor was failing in the production environment.

words.shortDefinition: The group of components that manage the Kubernetes cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: The brain of Kubernetes — decides where to schedule pods, stores cluster state, exposes the API. Nodes are workers, control plane is management.
words.longDefinition: It makes global decisions about the cluster (for example, scheduling), as well as detecting and responding to cluster events (for example, starting up a new pod). Components include etcd, api-server, and scheduler.
words.connector: Think of control plane like air traffic control — manages where planes (pods) land on runways (nodes), workers don't make those decisions.
words.techContext: In managed services like EKS or GKE, the cloud provider manages the Control Plane for you, so you only see the worker nodes.
words.exampleSentence: The cluster was unresponsive because the Control Plane's API server was overwhelmed by too many requests.

words.shortDefinition: A consistent and highly-available key value store used as Kubernetes' backing store.
words.simpleDefinition: Kubernetes' database — stores every resource's state (pods, services, config). Lose etcd, lose the cluster.
words.longDefinition: It stores all cluster data, including the state of every resource. If etcd is lost, the entire cluster configuration is gone. It uses the Raft consensus algorithm to ensure data consistency.
words.connector: Think of etcd like the master ledger in a bank — every account (resource) and balance (state) is recorded there, the single source of truth.
words.techContext: Etcd is the 'source of truth' for the entire cluster. Backing up etcd is the most important part of Kubernetes disaster recovery.
words.exampleSentence: The scheduler reads from etcd to find out which nodes have enough capacity to host new pods.
words.etymology: Named after the '/etc' directory in Unix (config) + 'd' (distributed).

words.shortDefinition: A network proxy that runs on each node in your cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: Runs on every node, handles routing traffic from Service IPs to the actual pod IPs behind them.
words.longDefinition: Maintains network rules on nodes. These network rules allow network communication to your Pods from network sessions inside or outside of your cluster. It implements the Kubernetes Service concept.
words.connector: Think of kube-proxy like a switchboard operator — routes calls from the Service's phone number to whichever pod (person) is available.
words.techContext: Kube-proxy is responsible for routing traffic from a Service's virtual IP to the actual IP of one of its backend pods.
words.exampleSentence: We investigated a networking delay and found that kube-proxy was struggling to update iptables rules on a very large node.

words.shortDefinition: Container Network Interface; a standard for configuring network interfaces in containers.
words.simpleDefinition: A standard for how network plugins connect pods — Kubernetes doesn't care how, just that it follows the CNI spec. Calico, Flannel, Cilium all implement CNI.
words.longDefinition: A CNCF project that consists of a specification and libraries for writing plugins to configure network interfaces in Linux containers. K8s uses CNI plugins (like Calico, Flannel, or Cilium) to provide Pod networking.
words.connector: Think of CNI like USB — a standard interface, different vendors (Calico, Cilium) implement it, Kubernetes just plugs in whichever one you choose.
words.techContext: We chose the Cilium CNI for our cluster because it provides advanced security features using eBPF.
words.exampleSentence: The cluster initialization failed because the CNI plugin wasn't properly configured to assign IP addresses to pods.

words.shortDefinition: Used to determine if a container is ready to start accepting traffic.
words.simpleDefinition: A health check that tells Kubernetes 'this pod is ready for traffic' — failing probes get removed from Service load balancing until they pass.
words.longDefinition: If a readiness probe fails, the endpoints controller removes the Pod's IP address from all Services that match the Pod. This ensures traffic is only sent to healthy, fully-initialized containers.
words.connector: Think of readiness probe like a kitchen's 'ready to serve' light — chef (pod) says 'not ready yet', waiters (Service) don't send orders until the light turns green.
words.techContext: I added a readiness probe that checks the '/health' endpoint of our app to prevent users from hitting a pod while it's still loading data into cache.
words.exampleSentence: The deployment took longer than expected because the pods were failing their readiness probes during initialization.

codeExample.code:
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10

words.shortDefinition: Used to determine if a container is still running and healthy.
words.simpleDefinition: A health check that says 'is this pod alive or frozen?' — fails? Kubernetes kills and restarts the pod.
words.longDefinition: If a liveness probe fails, the kubelet kills the container, and the container is subjected to its restart policy. This is used to automatically catch deadlocks where an application is running but unable to make progress.
words.connector: Think of liveness probe like a watchdog timer — if the app freezes and stops responding to pings, the watchdog kills it and reboots.
words.techContext: The liveness probe saved us last night by automatically restarting a pod that had frozen due to a memory leak.
words.exampleSentence: Be careful with liveness probes; if they are too aggressive, they might restart a healthy pod that is just temporarily under heavy load.

words.shortDefinition: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler; automatically scales the number of Pods in a deployment.
words.simpleDefinition: Automatically adds or removes pods based on CPU/memory usage or custom metrics — traffic spike? More pods. Quiet? Fewer pods.
words.longDefinition: Automatically scales the number of Pods in a replication controller, deployment, replica set, or stateful set based on observed CPU utilization (or, with custom metrics support, on some other application-provided metrics).
words.connector: Think of HPA like a thermostat for capacity — keeps CPU at ~70% by adding pods when hot, removing pods when cool.
words.techContext: We configured the HPA to maintain an average CPU usage of 70% across all pods in the web tier.
words.exampleSentence: The HPA automatically scaled our deployment from 2 to 10 pods within minutes of the marketing email being sent.

words.shortDefinition: Custom Resource Definition; a way to extend Kubernetes with your own API objects.
words.simpleDefinition: Create your own Kubernetes object types — like 'Database' or 'Certificate' — that work with kubectl just like built-in resources.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to define your own custom resources (like 'Database' or 'Certificate') that behave like built-in K8s objects. Combined with a custom controller, this forms the 'Operator' pattern.
words.connector: Think of CRDs like defining a custom class in programming — Kubernetes ships with Pod, Service, etc., CRDs let you add your own types.
words.techContext: Most service meshes and monitoring tools use CRDs to allow you to configure them using standard 'kubectl' commands.
words.exampleSentence: We defined a CRD for our internal internal deployment pipeline to allow developers to request new environments using YAML.
